analogy: Think of "South Asia" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview In reading about world politics, we frequently encounter the terms ‘security’ or ‘national security’. Often, they are used to stop debate and discussion. We hear that an issue is a security issue and that it is vital for the well-being of the country.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
South Asia is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that o verview In reading about world politics, we frequently encounter the terms ‘security’ or ‘national security’.

The text explains that often, they are used to stop debate and discussion.

The text explains that we hear that an issue is a security issue and that it is vital for the well-being of the country.

The text explains that the implication is that it is too important or secret to be debated and discussed openly.

The text explains that we see movies in which everything surrounding ‘national security’ is shadowy and dangerous.

The text explains that security seems to be something that is not the business of the ordinary citizen.

The text explains that in a democracy, surely this cannot be the case.

The text explains that as citizens of a democracy, we need to know more about the term security.

The text explains that it introduces two different ways of looking at security and highlights the importance of keeping in mind different contexts or situations which determine our view of security.

The text explains that chapter 5 Security in the Contemporary World The concern about human security was reflected in the 1994 UNDP’s Human Development Report, which contends, “the concept of security has for too long been interpreted narrowly… It has been more related to nation states than people… Forgotten were the legitimate concerns of ordinary people who sought security in their daily lives.” The images above show various forms of security threats.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 64 What is security?

The text explains that at its most basic, security implies freedom from threats.

The text explains that human existence and the life of a country are full of threats.

The text explains that does that mean that every single threat counts as a security threat?

The text explains that every time a person steps out of his or her house, there is some degree of threat to their existence and way of life.

The text explains that our world would be saturated with security issues if we took such a broad view of what is threatening.

The text explains that those who study security, therefore, generally say that only those things that threaten ‘core values’ should be regarded as being of interest in discussions of security.

The text explains that the core values of ordinary women and men in the street?

The text explains that do governments, on behalf of citizens, always have the same notion of core values as the ordinary citizen?

The text explains that furthermore, when we speak of threats to core values, how intense should the threats be?

The text explains that every time another country does something or fails to do something, this may damage the core values of one’s country.

The text explains that s o w e a r e b r o u g h t t o a conclusion: security relates only to extremely dangerous threats— threats that could so endanger core values that those values would be damaged beyond repair if we did not do something to deal with the situation.

The text explains that the source of this danger is another country which by threatening military action endangers the core values of sovereignty, independence and territorial integrity.

The text explains that since these problems are global in nature, international cooperation is vital, even though it is difficult to achieve. neW sources of threats The non-traditional conceptions— both human security and global security—focus on the changing nature of threats to security.

The text explains that global poverty is another source of insecurity.

The text explains that 17% Europe 16% Americas 11% Asia and Pacific 30% Africa 26% Middle East and North Africa Source: http://www.unhcr.org Where the world’s displaced people are being hosted Refugees in the world (2017)

The text explains that there is a pressure on the democratically elected governments to combine economic growth with human development.

The text explains that they had an uninterrupted access to abundant natural resources available in the region.

The text explains that gradually, people from different regions started coming to this region because of the abundant natural resources and water.

The text explains that each village started making claims over natural resources including the boundary of their respective settlement.

The text explains that inhabitants of Kotabagh argued for a greater share in natural resources, as they were the first settlers.

The text explains that all four villages disagreed with each other’s demands and continued to use the resources as they wished.

The text explains that ideas for the Teacher  Link the villages to nations and connect to the problems of security (threat to geographical territory/ access to natural resources/insurgency, and so on).  Talk about the observations made during the negotiation and explain how similarly the nations behave while negotiating on related issues.  The activity could be concluded by making reference to some of the current security issues between and among nations.

The text explains that is terrorism a traditional or non-traditional threat to security?

The text explains that what are the choices available to a state when its security is threatened, according to the traditional security perspective?

The text explains that what are the objectives of military alliances?

The text explains that give an example of a functioning military alliance with its specific objectives.

The text explains that rapid environmental degradation is causing a serious threat to security.

The text explains that nuclear weapons as deterrence or defence have limited usage against contemporary security threats to states.

The text explains that looking at the Indian scenario, what type of security has been given priority in India, traditional or non-traditional?

The text explains that what examples could you cite to substantiate the argument?

The text explains that read the cartoon below and write a short note in favour or against the connection between war and terrorism depicted in this cartoon.

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In responding to the threat of war, a government has three basic choices: to surrender; to prevent the other side from attacking by promising to raise... (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Governments
schemes.point: Governments may choose to surrender when actually confronted by war, but they will not advertise this as the policy of the country. (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Therefore
schemes.point: Therefore, security policy is concer ned with preventing war, which is called deterrence, and with limiting or ending war, which is called defence. (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Traditional
schemes.point: Traditional security policy has a third component called balance of power. (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Institutional reference
schemes.point: A f o u r t h a n d r e l a t e d component of traditional security policy is alliance building. (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch5 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1994 UNDP’s Human Development Report, which contends, and STEPS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to South Asia; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain South Asia through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1994 UNDP’s Human Development Report, which contends, and STEPS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
